title: Chapter 1: The Corpus
text:
Corpus selection and preperation ook nog over samen doen en region toevoegen
The corpus for this analysis is a combination of two playlists from spotify: “West Side Classics” and “East Coast Classics”. These playlists were chosen, because they were very popular on spotify, with 141.754 and 40.882 likes respectivally. As there is no golden standard for what songs are considered “West Coast” or “East Coast”, this method of popularity was used, which is similair to methods used by Boros (2025) and Gilbers et al. (2019).
In the West Coast (W.C.) playlist, some popular East Coast (E.C.) artist appeard. To mitigate this problem, artist that were in both datasets were noted and placed in their corresponding Coast. The artist ‘Outkast’ also appeared in both datasets and was removed as Outkast originated from Atlanta (not W.C. nor E.C.). Moreover, “West Side Classics” contained 84 songs, while “East Coast Classics” contained 177 after the previous step of moving around songs. As this is a difference of more that half of the songs, the E.C. set was downsampled to the W.C. set, both now containing 84 songs.


Track selection
To make analysis about track level information, two tracks per coast were selected. These songs needed to be representative for the range of music of every coast. To select these without randomly choosing two, one is the most respresentative for that coast and the other is a track distinctive for its coast, far from the other coast and not an outlier in its own coast. The latter was chosen so that the most distinctive track from a coast is not a general outlier (i.e. the most distinctive song for a coast could be a song that does not represent either coasts). First, all numerical features except “Popularity” (as this measure is changing) from the Spotify API were normalised. Second, the centroid (i.e. the mean over all features) of the data was calculated. To select the most representative song, the euclidean distance from each song to the centroid was calculated, and the song with the smallest distance was selected. To select the most distinctive song, the euclidean distance from each song to the other coast’s centroid was calculated. From that, the distance to its own coast’s centroid was subtracted. The song that was the furthest from the other coast’s centroid, while being close to its own coast’s centroid was selected. The songs that were selected are:




East Coast


West Coast




Representative


Distinct


Representative


Distinct






Give me the Loot by The Notorious B.I.G.


C.R.E.A.M.(Cash Rules Everything Around Me) by Wu-Tang Clan


Bitch Please by Snoop Dogg and Xzibit


Without me by Eminem

title: Chapter 3: Chroma features
text:
Chromagrams of all four songs, structured in per song, per side, total. So first a song and analyses, then of the other from that side, then comparing those. Then the other side and concluding what we see in total

Listen to Give me the Loot and Bitch Plase















The difference between these two songs is really striking. “Give Me The Loot” is packed with sounds happening all over the place, while “Bitch Please” is much more straightforward and stable.
In GMTL, you hear a beat, a two voices, three or four samples and record scratches, all in 10 seconds! Eventhough this is an extreme window in the track, it illustrates the richness of the sound of this song. When listening to other parts of the song, you can hear a stable sample and Biggies voice. The sample form the basis of the song, hence the quite stable line at B-flat. Moreover, throughout the song, there are a lot of yellow spikes seen btween C-sharp and F-sharp. This is explainable by Biggies voice. It quite deep from itself (hence the lower notes) and has some spikes to higher notes, being the highest at the 150-180 seconds mark.
On the other hand in BP, the song is more melodically stable. There is a beat and a sample that stays stable throughout the whole song. In the 2:00-2:10 window, you can hear two more samples that both fade in and out throughout the song, a long sliding synth and a synthesized triangle sound. These samples follow the same melodical pattern. The stair-like pattern you hear from these samples, cannot be seen in the chromagram. What you can see, is that were the sliding synth is prominent (120-140 seconds and from 180 untill the end of the song), the B and F-sharp pitches are more yellow, while the rest of the pitches get darker.









When comparing the Self-Similarity matrices, both songs show similar base patterns of beats and samples, but with different intensities. GMTL displays more bright yellow lines indicating greater pitch variation, while BP shows less intense patterns, reflecting its more stable melodic structure.


Listen to C.R.E.A.M and Without Me















The difference between C.R.E.A.M (CREAM) and Without Me (WM) is a lot less aparent. The Pitches in CREAM remain very stable, being most prominent in F-sharp, A, and C-sharp. The melody throughout the whole track is essentially the same except for the 25 second intro. Impurities in the pitch classes can be explained by many different voices, as Wu-Tang Clan is a big rap group.
WM is also quite a rich songs, but stays more stable in pitch class than GMTL we saw earlier. It’s most intense in G and D, and also periodically in F. There is a melodical riff in the background (a oboe like sound) and goes down in steps periodically. This can not really be seen in the chromagram, which can be explained due to the fact that it is quite a background sample. Eminems voice, for example, is much more prominent.









The difference in the SSM’s is more clear. The SSM of CREAM has quite some repeating blocks, alternated with bright lines. This is, again, only explainable by the different voices heard.
The SSM of WM quite bright allover. You can see some blocks, but they are all quite small. Eminem has a very prominent, acticulated voice, explaining the scale of differences.

title: Week 11
text:
For this weeks assignment, I have implemented two KNN models. One was trained on the orginal spotify features and one was trained on the Spotify features and 3 extra features. These are:
I found these extra features from a hit-song-predictor project and they seemed like a good addition to the features. First, energy_dancebility gives and indication of a ‘party factor’, for tracks that are both energetic and danceble.Second, Valence x energy gives an indication of positive vibes and upbeatness. Last, mood score indicates something about the overall emotional tone.

title: Week 11
section: KNN Classification Results without feature engineering
text:
KNN Classification Results without feature engineering
















Best k: 1
Here, you see a plot of the KNN prediction constructed with PCA and a confusion matrix of the predictions. In the plot, the ground truth is indicated with the shape and the classifiction with the color. The plot shows that the model is able to capture some of the variance in the data, but there is a lot of overlap between the two classes, indicating that the model is not performing very well. The confusion matrix confirms this, showing that there are a lot of misclassifications.

title: Week 11
section: KNN Classification Results with feature engineering
text:
KNN Classification Results with feature engineering
















Best k: 1
The model with the extra features show a non-significant improvement on the classification. That could be, because there is little difference between east and west coast, but it could also be due to the fact that the features add little extra as they are primarily focused around enery and valence. If these features are not imporant at all, adding additional features for this does not help.

title: Week 9
text:
Homework week 9

Introduction
In the late 1990s and early 2000s, American hip-hop was marked by a West Coast vs. East Coast feud, with fans strongly aligned to one side. The rivalry ultimately led to the deaths of Tupac and Biggie. For this week, I made visualisations for the song ‘Bitch please’ by Snoop Dogg and Xzibit. I chose this song as it is statistically the most west-coast, meaning its audio profile sits closest to the overall average of all West Coast tracks (Only considering spotifies features).


Chromagram



Bitch Please chromagram


In this chromagram, you can see that the song is mostly in the keys B and F#, with G being high in the beginning of the song, but becoming less prominant later. This repetitiveness of pitches (i.e. the B and F#) can be explained by the fact that most rap songs have a looped sample, which repeat throughout the whole song


SelfSM timbre features



Bitch Please SSM


In this self-similarity matrix, you can see that the song features a highly dense, checkerboard-like pattern across the entire track, with consistent horizontal and vertical stripes indicating regular, repeating intervals of high similarity. This intense repetitiveness in the song’s timbre can be explained by the fact that most rap songs, particularly from this era of West Coast hip-hop, are built on a continuous, looped instrumental beat, meaning the underlying drum textures, basslines, and instrumentation repeat almost entirely unchanged throughout the whole song.

title: Week 11
text:
Homework week 11
In the late 1990s and early 2000s, American hip-hop was marked by a West Coast vs. East Coast feud, with fans strongly aligned to one side. The rivalry ultimately led to the deaths of Tupac and Biggie. For this week, I made a chordagram for the song ‘Give me the Loot’ by The Notorius B.I.G. I chose this song as it is statistically the most east-coast, meaning its audio profile sits closest to the overall average of all East Coast tracks (Only considering spotify’s features) (See here for more information).

Listen to Give me the Loot by The Notorius B.I.G.












In this graph, you see both the tempogram of Give Me The Loot made with Autocorrelation (ATC) and with the Discrete Fourier Transform. In both graphs, the lines are straight, indicating a steady rythm throughout the song. The tempo is around 90 BPM, which is a typical tempo for hip-hop/rap. In the ATC tempogram, the brightest line is prominent at around 45 BPM. In the DFT tempogram, the lines show at 90 BPM and multiples of that. Taking both the 45 BPM of the ATC and the 90 BPM of the DFT graph, we can conclude that the rythm of the song lies at around 90 BPM (45 BPM is half beat). Moreover, what stands out is that the tempo is prominent in the first 50 seconds, after which is fades out. If you listen closely in the song, the voice of Biggie get more prominent around the 50 second mark, resulting in less of a part of the output showing the beat, explaining this fading out phenomenon. Around the 100 seconds mark, there is a section where no tempo seems present. As you can hear in the song,the drums and samples are off around this time resulting in this gap in the tempo. In conclusion, this graph shows that this song has a steady beat with a tempo of around 90 BPM. This boom-bap-beat and tempo form the basis of the rapping, done by Biggies with his prominent voice.

title: Week 10
text:
Homework week 10
In the late 1990s and early 2000s, American hip-hop was marked by a West Coast vs. East Coast feud, with fans strongly aligned to one side. The rivalry ultimately led to the deaths of Tupac and Biggie. For this week, I made a chordagram for the song ‘Give me the Loot’ by The Notorius B.I.G. I chose this song as it is statistically the most east-coast, meaning its audio profile sits closest to the overall average of all East Coast tracks (Only considering spotify’s features). #### Listen to the song   









This is a chordagram of Give me the Loot by The Notorius B.I.G. In the chrodagram, you can see that the overall color is greenish, and that there are a lot of soft horizontal blue lines. The fact that the overall color is greenish and, for example, not bright yellow in most parts, tells us that the sound of this song is very rich. When listening to the song, there is a soft background sample overlayed with harder drums and ofcourse Biggies prominent voice. Together with that, there are a lot of sounds going in and out of the song: alarm like souds, record scratches and other voices. In the graph, you can also see a lot of vertical lines. Oftentimes in the song, you hear the samples and drums stop, only keeping Biggies voice. These stops correspond to the vertical lines, as there is a sudden stop of a lot of chords.

title: Chapter 5: Timbre
text:
Cepstrograms of all four songs, structured in per song, per side, total. So first a song and analyses, then of the other from that side, then comparing those. Then the other side and concluding what we see in total.

Listen to Give me the Loot and Bitch Please















The cepstrograms reveal distinct differences in timbre complexity between these tracks. “Give Me The Loot” displays a diverse range of MFCC values with multiple peaks across different frequencies, reflecting the sample-rich, layered production characteristic of East Coast hip-hop. “Bitch Please” shows more concentrated spectral energy, particularly in the lower MFCC coefficients, representing a more straightforward, groove-oriented West Coast sound.


Listen to C.R.E.A.M and Without Me















Comparing C.R.E.A.M and Without Me, we observe that both tracks maintain relatively stable timbral characteristics throughout. C.R.E.A.M exhibits consistent MFCC distribution, reflecting Wu-Tang Clan’s cohesive sample-based production style. Without Me shows periodic variations in spectral content, corresponding to shifts in the vocal delivery and instrumental background, demonstrating Eminem’s more dynamic use of timbre as a production element.

title: Chapter 2: Exploratoring the features
text:
Before diving into songs specifically, we will look at whether there are differences on a coast level. To do this, we analyse a plot containing density-plots () of both coast overlayed with eachother. This way, we can clearly see where the coasts’ features overlap and where not.









Overall, the distributions are quite similiar. Both coasts’ song distributions have relativaly the same spread of duration, energy, instrumentalness, key, liveness, loudness, mode, tempo and valence. Next to that, there are three features where the distributions differ. First, acousticness show a clear seperation. W.C. songs are heavily skewed to less acoustic songs, while E.C. have a more spread acousticness distribution. Second, W.C. songs are more skewed toward a higher danceability than E.C. songs. Last, speechiness in W.C. songs is skewed to the left relative to E.C.
In addition, what is interstring to see is that the instrumentallness of all songs is zero (due to the fact that it is mostly rap, hip-hop and samples), while the speechiness remains also relatively low (around 0.3). This combination indicates that the songs have a more musical beat while still revoling around vocals, describing a rythmic speech style which resonates with rap.

title: Chapter 7: Clustering

XGBoost Model
XGBoost (eXtreme Gradient Boosting) was trained to classify tracks as East Coast or West Coast hip-hop, using Bayesian hyperparameter optimization to find the best configuration.
